startScreenOptions.title: AN28.1-AN28.9 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 3 embedded questions for AN28.1-AN28.9.

interactions.label: A 60-year-old man presents with a stroke affecting the right lower face. He cann

params.question: A 60-year-old man presents with a stroke affecting the right lower face. He cannot smile on the right side, but he can raise both eyebrows normally. Why is the forehead spared in this upper motor neuron lesion?

answers.text: The frontalis is supplied by the trigeminal nerve, not the facial nerve

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The frontalis is a muscle of facial expression and is supplied exclusively by the facial nerve (CN VII). The trigeminal nerve provides sensory supply to the forehead, not motor.

answers.text: The frontalis receives bilateral cortical motor representation, so the intact hemisphere compensates

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Both halves of the motor cortex project to the frontalis. When one hemisphere is damaged, the contralateral cortex maintains frontalis function, sparing the forehead.

answers.text: The frontalis is a muscle of mastication, not a muscle of facial expression

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The frontalis is a muscle of facial expression derived from the second pharyngeal arch. Muscles of mastication (masseter, temporalis, pterygoids) are supplied by the mandibular division of the trigeminal nerve.

answers.text: The temporal branch of the facial nerve recovers faster than other branches after a stroke

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Forehead sparing is not a recovery phenomenon. It is present immediately in an UMN lesion because of bilateral cortical innervation, not because of differential nerve recovery.

overallFeedback.feedback: The correct answer is bilateral cortical representation. Both hemispheres supply the upper face, so one hemisphere compensates when the other is damaged. Forehead sparing is the hallmark sign that separates stroke (UMN) from Bell's palsy (LMN).

Exactly right. Bilateral cortical representation of the frontalis is the key to distinguishing UMN from LMN facial palsy. In a lower motor neuron lesion such as Bell's palsy, the entire ipsilateral face is affected, including the forehead.

metadata.title: A 60-year-old man presents with a stroke

interactions.label: A patient presents with numbness over the lower lip and chin following a mandibu

params.question: A patient presents with numbness over the lower lip and chin following a mandibular fracture. Which nerve has been damaged, and through which foramen does it exit?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The infraorbital nerve is a branch of the maxillary division (V2) and supplies the cheek, upper lip, and side of the nose — not the lower lip and chin.
Correct. The mental nerve, a terminal branch of the mandibular division (V3), exits through the mental foramen in the body of the mandible to supply the lower lip and chin.
Incorrect. The supraorbital nerve is a branch of the ophthalmic division (V1) and supplies the forehead and upper eyelid, not the lower face.
Incorrect. The auriculotemporal nerve supplies the skin in front of the ear and the temple. The foramen ovale transmits the mandibular nerve trunk itself, not a cutaneous branch directly.

overallFeedback.feedback: The correct answer is the mental nerve through the mental foramen. Remember the three exit points: supraorbital notch (V1 — forehead), infraorbital foramen (V2 — cheek and upper lip), mental foramen (V3 — lower lip and chin).
Well done. The mental nerve (from V3) exits through the mental foramen to supply the lower lip and chin. It is vulnerable in mandibular fractures, making this a common clinical presentation.

interactions.label: During a parotidectomy, the surgeon must identify three structures within the pa

params.question: During a parotidectomy, the surgeon must identify three structures within the parotid gland that are arranged in a consistent deep-to-superficial order. Which sequence correctly describes this arrangement?

answers.text: Facial nerve → retromandibular vein → external carotid artery

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. This is the reverse of the correct order. The facial nerve is the most superficial structure within the parotid gland, not the deepest.

answers.text: External carotid artery → retromandibular vein → facial nerve

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. From deep to superficial: the external carotid artery lies deepest (dividing into the superficial temporal and maxillary arteries), the retromandibular vein is intermediate, and the facial nerve is most superficial.

answers.text: Retromandibular vein → external carotid artery → facial nerve

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The external carotid artery lies deeper than the retromandibular vein within the parotid gland, not the other way round.

answers.text: External carotid artery → facial nerve → retromandibular vein

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The facial nerve is the most superficial of the three structures, not intermediate. The retromandibular vein lies between the artery and the nerve.

overallFeedback.feedback: The correct order from deep to superficial is: external carotid artery → retromandibular vein → facial nerve. The nerve's superficial location explains why it is most vulnerable during parotidectomy and must be meticulously preserved.

Correct. From deep to superficial: artery, vein, nerve. The facial nerve's superficial position makes it the structure most at risk during parotid surgery, which is why the surgeon identifies it first at the stylomastoid foramen and preserves it carefully.
